content.problemStatement:
This is the easy version of the problem. The only difference between this version and the hard version is the constraints: here, the sum of NNN and the sum of MMM across all tests both won't exceed 200020002000.

There are N+MN+MN+M points lying on the 2D coordinate plane. NNN of these points lie on the positive xxx-axis (i.e. are of the form (x,0)(x, 0)(x,0) for x>0x \gt 0x>0), and the other MMM lie on the positive yyy-axis (i.e. are of the form (0,y)(0, y)(0,y) for y>0y \gt 0y>0).

It is guaranteed that N‚Č•3N \ge 3N‚Č•3 and M‚Č•3M \ge 3M‚Č•3. Further, all N+MN+MN+M points are at distinct positions.

You must give each of these N+MN+MN+M points a color: either red or blue.

The only constraint is that each axis must have at least one point colored red and at least one point colored blue ‚ÄĒ aside from that, the coloring can be freely decided.

Define the monochromatic number of a coloring to be the **maximum area** of a **triangle** formed by some triple of points that all have the same color (either all three are red or all three are blue).

Your task is find the minimum possible monochromatic number across all possible valid colorings of the points.

If the answer is AAA, you must output 2A2A2A.

It can be proved that under the constraints of this problem, 2A2A2A is always an integer.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM ‚ÄĒ the number of points on the xxx-axis and yyy-axis, respectively.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers x1,x2,‚Ä¶,xNx_1, x_2, \ldots, x_Nx1‚Äč,x2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,xN‚Äč, meaning that the points on the xxx-axis are (x1,0),‚Ä¶,(xN,0)(x_1, 0), \ldots, (x_N, 0)(x1‚Äč,0),‚Ä¶,(xN‚Äč,0).
The third line contains MMM space-separated integers y1,y2,‚Ä¶,yMy_1, y_2, \ldots, y_My1‚Äč,y2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,yM‚Äč, meaning that the points on the yyy-axis are (0,y1),‚Ä¶,(0,yM)(0, y_1), \ldots, (0, y_M)(0,y1‚Äč),‚Ä¶,(0,yM‚Äč).




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the value 2A2A2A, where AAA is the minimum possible monochromatic area across all valid colorings.
constraints: The sum of NNN and the sum of MMM over all test cases each won't exceed 200020002000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: One optimal solution is as follows:

Color points (1,0),(0,2),(0,3)(1, 0), (0, 2), (0, 3)(1,0),(0,2),(0,3) red.
There is then only one triangle with all vertices red, namely the one with the three above vertices.
It can be verified that this triangle has an area of 0.50.50.5.
Color points (2,0),(3,0),(0,1)(2, 0), (3, 0), (0, 1)(2,0),(3,0),(0,1) blue.
Again, there is then only one triangle with all vertices blue, and it can be verified to have an area of 0.50.50.5.

The maximum monochrome area of this configuration is hence max‚Ā°(0.5,0.5)=0.5\max(0.5, 0.5) = 0.5max(0.5,0.5)=0.5.
It can be proved that this is optimal.
Since we are asked to output twice the optimal answer, we output 2‚čÖ0.5=12\cdot 0.5 = 12‚čÖ0.5=1.
